tasks.task_description:
Perform a comparative audit of “search” endpoints and their pagination strategies across three OpenAPI specifications: openai, github, and cloudflare.  
1. For each spec (openai, github, cloudflare), call getApiOverview to retrieve the full list of paths and operations.  
2. From each overview, identify every operation whose operationId or path contains the substring “search”.  
3. For each identified search operation, call getApiOperation to fetch its details. Extract its parameters and response schema fields to determine if it supports pagination. Identify pagination style (page-based via page/page_size, cursor-based via cursor/next_cursor, or none), parameter names, types, required flags, and default values. Also note any response fields related to pagination (e.g., next_page, next_cursor).  
4. If in any spec none of the search operations support pagination, then:  
   a. Re-use getApiOverview on that spec and identify operations with “list” in their operationId or path.  
   b. For each list operation, call getApiOperation and extract the same pagination data as above.  
5. Consolidate findings into a comparative table (JSON array) with one entry per operation, containing: 
   • spec (openai/github/cloudflare)  
   • operationId  
   • path  
   • paginationMechanism ("page-based", "cursor-based", or "none")  
   • parameters: [{ name, in, type, required, default }]  
   • responsePaginationFields: [field names]  
6. Highlight any spec where search endpoints lack built-in pagination and must fall back to list endpoints, and compare the pagination consistency across all three specs.

tasks.dependency_analysis:
Step 1 (Sequential): Use OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOverview with id=openai/github/cloudflare to retrieve each spec’s list of operations.  
Step 2 (Local Filtering): Filter each overview result for operations containing “search” in operationId or path.  
Step 3 (Parallel Detailed Fetch): For each filtered operation across the three specs, invoke OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOperation to obtain its full parameter and response schema.  
Step 4 (Data Extraction & Branching): Parse each operation’s parameters to detect pagination parameters and parse response schemas for pagination fields. Record pagination style.  
Step 5 (Decision Point): If for a given spec none of its search operations support pagination, trigger a conditional sub-workflow: call getApiOverview again on that spec, filter for “list” operations, then call getApiOperation on each to extract pagination data.  
Step 6 (Aggregation): Combine data from both search and conditional list operations into a unified comparative report.  
Key Dependencies: getApiOverview outputs feed filtered operation identifiers into getApiOperation. The presence/absence of pagination in search outputs drives a branch to fetch and analyze list operations. Finally, all parsed results are merged into a single output structure.

tasks.task_description:
Perform a cross-API specification audit comparing the OpenAI and GitHub OpenAPI specs. 1) Call OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOverview with id="openai" to retrieve the full list of operations and securitySchemes. 2) From the OpenAI overview, select every operation whose operationId contains the substring "create"; for each of these operations, call OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOperation to extract the requestBody.schema.required properties and count how many required fields each operation has. 3) Call OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOverview with id="github" to retrieve the full list of operations and securitySchemes. 4) From the GitHub overview, select every operation whose path begins with "/repos"; for each of these, call OpenAPI Explorer:getApiOperation to extract all path, query, and requestBody parameters and count how many are required. 5) Extract and list each security scheme type (e.g. apiKey, oauth2) defined in both specs. 6) Compare the two specs and produce a consolidated JSON report containing:  
  - openai.authSchemes: list of security scheme names and types  
  - openai.createOperations: array of { operationId, requiredParamCount }  
  - github.authSchemes: list of security scheme names and types  
  - github.repoOperations: array of { path, requiredParamCount }  
  - crossComparison.operationsWithHighParamCount: list of operations (with spec and identifier) having more than 3 required parameters  
  - crossComparison.commonSecurityTypes: intersection of security scheme types between OpenAI and GitHub specs

tasks.task_description:
Comprehensive Reactor X Startup Readiness Check: You are provided with a set of sensor readings taken just before startup of Reactor X. Each sensor reading has a specified threshold in SI units. Your job is to:
1. Verify that all needed unit types are supported by calling list_supported_units (unit_type null to get all types).
2. Perform a batched conversion of all 14 sensor readings into their SI threshold units using convert_batch.  For each request include: value, from_unit, to_unit, conversion_type, and a unique request_id.
3. Parse the batch response and for each sensor compare the converted value to its SI threshold:
   - If converted_value ≥ threshold_value, status is PASS.
   - If converted_value < threshold_value, status is FAIL.
4. For any sensor that FAILs, invoke the corresponding individual conversion tool (e.g., convert_temperature for temperature failures, convert_pressure for pressure failures, etc.) with the same input parameters as in the batch to cross-validate the conversion result.
5. Produce a final JSON report listing each sensor with fields: sensor_name, original_reading (value + unit), converted_value (with unit), threshold_value (with unit), status (PASS/FAIL), cross_validation (object with batch_value and individual_value if cross-validation was performed).

Sensors and thresholds:
1. inlet_temperature: 350 °F → threshold 150 °C (temperature)
2. inlet_pressure: 50 psi → threshold 350 kPa (pressure)
3. reactor_length: 10 ft → threshold 5 m (length)
4. catalyst_weight: 500 lb → threshold 200 kg (mass)
5. tank_volume: 2000 gallon (imperial) → threshold 8 m³ (volume)
6. data_buffer: 2 gigabyte → threshold 1500 megabyte (computer_data)
7. heat_exchanger_area: 1000 ft² → threshold 90 m² (area)
8. motor_power: 50 horsepower → threshold 40 kilowatt (power)
9. reaction_time: 2 hours → threshold 6000 seconds (time)
10. valve_angle: 0.25 turns → threshold 45 degrees (angle)
11. conveyor_speed: 2 meters/second → threshold 4000 feet/minute (speed)
12. valve_force: 500 pounds force → threshold 2000 newtons (force)
13. fluid_density: 128 pounds per cubic foot → threshold 2000 kilograms per cubic meter (density)
14. fuel_energy: 10000 Btu → threshold 12000 kilojoule (energy)

Produce the report exactly as specified; do not request further information.

tasks.task_description:
You are evaluating the performance of a high-altitude research drone during an upcoming 2 hour 30 minute test flight. All original measurements are in U.S. customary units. Using the provided unit conversion tools, you must:

1. Verify supported units for angle conversions by calling list_supported_units for unit_type "angle".  
   - If the unsupported unit "grads" is not listed, fall back to "gons".  

2. Convert the following raw measurements to SI units:
   • Engine inlet temperature: 500 °F → Kelvin  
   • Engine outlet temperature: 300 °F → Kelvin  
   • Propeller pitch angle: 15 degrees → choose target unit from step 1 ("gons")  
   • Cruise speed: 60 knots → meters per second  
   • Cruise altitude: 10 000 feet → meters  
   • Wing span: 15 feet → meters  
   • Wing area: 1 200 square inches → square meters  
   • Cargo bay pressure: 50 psi → pascals  
   • Takeoff thrust: 3 000 pounds-force → newtons  

3. Convert the following storage, energy, and power metrics:
   • On-board log storage: 2 gigabytes → bytes  
   • Flight data downlink buffer: 10 megabytes → bytes  
   • Battery capacity: 5 kilowatt-hours → joules  
   • Average power draw (compute as battery capacity / flight duration): → compute in kilowatts, then convert kilowatts → horsepower  

4. Compute fuel usage metrics:
   • Fuel tank volume at start: 200 U.S. gallons → cubic meters  
   • Fuel density: 810 grams per liter → kilograms per cubic meter  
   • Calculate total starting fuel mass in kilograms by multiplying the converted volume by the converted density.  
   • Decision point: if the starting fuel mass > 100 kg, convert that mass → tonnes; otherwise convert → pounds.  

5. Convert flight duration:
   • 2 hours 30 minutes → seconds  

6. Summarize all converted values and derived metrics in a single JSON object with clearly labeled fields: original_value, original_unit, converted_value, converted_unit. Include computed fields: average_power: { value, unit }, starting_fuel_mass: { value, unit }, and a note about which branch was taken at the fuel-mass decision.

You must use list_supported_units, convert_temperature, convert_angle, convert_speed, convert_length, convert_area, convert_pressure, convert_force, convert_volume, convert_density, convert_computer_data, convert_energy, convert_power, convert_time, and convert_mass. Implement a batch conversion for the temperature, pressure, storage, energy, and power conversions; if any batch item fails, fall back to the individual convert_* calls. All steps must be performed without asking for further input.
tasks.fuzzy_description:

Can you help me convert all of that—temperatures to kelvins, angle to gons, speed to m/s, lengths to meters, area to m², pressure to pascals, force to newtons, storage to bytes, energy to joules, compute the average power draw in kW then to horsepower, volume to m³, density to kg/m³, and time to seconds—then calculate the total starting fuel mass in kilograms and, if it ends up over 100 kg, report it in tonnes (otherwise in pounds)? tasks.dependency_analysis:
Inherent dependencies:
• list_supported_units → drives the choice of target unit for convert_angle.  
• convert_volume and convert_density → both feed into the starting fuel mass calculation, whose output drives a subsequent convert_mass call.  
• convert_energy and convert_time → their outputs are combined to compute average power, then fed into convert_power.
• convert_computer_data and convert_energy/convert_power share batch conversion for efficiency.

Scenario-based dependencies:
1. Initial unit check: list_supported_units(angle) determines whether to use "grads" (unsupported) or fallback to "gons" for convert_angle.
2. Batch conversion attempt: temperature, pressure, storage, energy, and power metrics are sent in one convert_batch call. On any failure, the system sequentially invokes the corresponding individual convert_* tool.
3. Fuel mass chain:
   a. convert_volume → yields volume_m3.  
   b. convert_density → yields density_kg_per_m3.  
   c. Compute fuel_mass_kg = volume_m3 × density_kg_per_m3.  
   d. Decision: if fuel_mass_kg > 100, branch to convert_mass → tonnes; else branch to convert_mass → pounds.
4. Power chain:
   a. convert_energy (5 kWh → J) and convert_time (2.5 h → s) run in parallel.  
   b. Compute average_power_kW = (5 kWh)/(2.5 h).  
   c. convert_power to horsepower.
5. Sequential and parallel flows:
   • Parallel: volume/density; energy/time; computer_data conversions.  
   • Sequential: batch → fallback; convert_volume & convert_density → mass decision → convert_mass.

No cross-server dependencies are required (all tools are on the Unit Converter server), but multiple conversion types are orchestrated to build derived metrics and decision branches.

tasks.task_description:
You are tasked with producing a comprehensive research dossier on major global climate change negotiation frameworks as described in Wikipedia. Follow these steps exactly, using only the provided Wikipedia tools:

1. Use Wikipedia:search_wikipedia with query="climate change negotiation frameworks" and limit=5. Collect the returned article titles into a list called frameworks.

2. For each title in frameworks, in parallel:
   a. Call Wikipedia:get_article with the title to fetch the full content.
   b. Call Wikipedia:get_sections with the title to retrieve the list of section titles.
   c. If the sections list contains a section titled "History":
        i. Call Wikipedia:summarize_article_section with title, section_title="History", max_length=150.
      Else:
        i. Call Wikipedia:get_summary with title.
   d. Call Wikipedia:get_links with title and count the number of returned links. Record this as link_count for that framework.

3. Identify the framework titled exactly "Paris Agreement". For that title:
   a. Call Wikipedia:summarize_article_for_query with title="Paris Agreement", query="emission reduction targets", max_length=200. Store the result as paris_emission_summary.
   b. Call Wikipedia:extract_key_facts with title="Paris Agreement", topic_within_article="emission reduction targets", count=5. Store the list as paris_emission_facts.

4. Identify the framework titled exactly "Kyoto Protocol". For that title:
   a. Call Wikipedia:get_related_topics with title="Kyoto Protocol", limit=5.
   b. If fewer than 5 related topics are returned, re-call Wikipedia:get_related_topics with title="Kyoto Protocol" and limit=10. Store the final list as kyoto_related_topics.

5. Cross-validate the paris_emission_facts against paris_emission_summary. Produce a list of any facts from paris_emission_facts not explicitly mentioned in paris_emission_summary; call this mismatches.

6. Compile and output a single JSON object with the following structure:
{
  "frameworks": [
    {"title": string, "summary": string, "link_count": integer}, ... up to 5 frameworks
  ],
  "paris_emission_summary": string,
  "paris_emission_facts": [string, … 5 items],
  "mismatches": [string, …],
  "kyoto_related_topics": [string, …]
}

Ensure you never request additional information and only use the specified Wikipedia tools in the order and logic described.

Then dive into the Paris Agreement: I’d like about a 200-word summary focused on its emission-reduction targets and five standout facts. tasks.dependency_analysis:
Inherent dependencies:
- search_wikipedia → get_article: search provides titles consumed by get_article.
- get_article → get_sections, get_links, get_summary: article retrieval enables downstream analysis.
- get_sections → summarize_article_section: section existence drives section-based summarization.
- summarize_article_for_query and extract_key_facts both require the same article title and generate cross-validation data.
- get_related_topics contains iterative dependency: if initial limit is insufficient, increase limit and rerun.

Scenario-based dependencies and data flow:
1. A single search determines the primary list of 5 frameworks (decision point).
2. For each framework, article content is fetched (parallel branches), then sections dictate whether to run section summarization or full summary (conditional workflow).
3. Links are counted independently for each framework (parallel, then aggregated).
4. The specific framework "Paris Agreement" triggers a deep dive: first a query-focused summary, then extraction of 5 key facts on the same topic. These two outputs are cross-validated to identify mismatches (cross-validation dependency).
5. The specific framework "Kyoto Protocol" triggers related topic discovery with an iterative loop: if the first call returns fewer than 5 topics, the limit is raised and the tool is re-invoked.
6. Final compilation requires combining parallel branches (framework summaries), decision-driven outputs (History vs full summary), iterative results (Kyoto Protocol), and cross-validated data (Paris mismatches).

Critical decision points:
- Presence of a "History" section per framework to choose summary path.
- Quantity of related topics for Kyoto Protocol to decide on re-calling the tool.
- Discrepancies between extracted key facts and summary sentences for Paris Agreement.

Sequential vs. parallel:
- Step 1 is strictly sequential (search → list of titles).
- Step 2 runs five parallel pipelines for each framework.
- Steps 3 and 4 are conditional branches on specific titles from that list.
- Step 5 merges and cross-validates outputs from steps 3a and 3b.
- Step 6 aggregates all results into the final JSON.

No external servers are used; all data flows exclusively through the Wikipedia tools provided, forming a tight dependency graph that must be followed to complete the task.

tasks.task_description:
Compare the environmental impact and relevant policy frameworks of two renewable energy technologies: “Solar energy” and “Wind power”.

Steps:
1. Search Wikipedia for “Solar energy renewable energy” and take the top result as the primary article for Solar.
2. Search Wikipedia for “Wind power renewable energy” and take the top result as the primary article for Wind.
3. For each technology article:
   a. Get the list of sections.
   b. If a section titled exactly “Environmental impact” or “Environmental impacts” exists, summarize that section (max_length = 200). Otherwise, generate a tailored summary of that article for the query “environmental impact” (max_length = 200).
   c. Extract the top 5 key facts from the article, focused on “Environmental impact”.
4. Compare the two sets of environmental key facts side by side in a table.
5. For each technology article, get up to 5 related topics; identify any policy or regulatory topics among them (e.g., “Feed-in tariff”, “Renewable energy policy”).
6. If no explicit policy-related topics appear, perform a fresh Wikipedia search for “renewable energy policy frameworks” and select the top result as the policy article.
7. Summarize the policy article for the query “incentives for solar and wind power” (max_length = 300).
8. Cross-validate by checking each technology article’s links: determine whether the policy article appears in their outbound links.
9. Finally, propose one additional renewable technology (from the related topics lists) to research next, and provide a 2-sentence rationale.

Expected output format:
{
  "solar_environmental_summary": "...",
  "wind_environmental_summary": "...",
  "solar_key_facts": ["fact1", …],
  "wind_key_facts": ["fact1", …],
  "comparison_table": [{"fact_index":1, "solar":"…", "wind":"…"}, …],
  "policy_article_title": "…",
  "policy_summary": "…",
  "solar_links_policy_present": true/false,
  "wind_links_policy_present": true/false,
  "recommended_next_technology": "…",
  "recommendation_rationale": "…"
}

tasks.task_description:
You are a research coordinator planning submissions for upcoming academic conferences. Using the Call for Papers:get_events tool, identify all conferences in Europe on "Artificial Intelligence" and "Data Privacy" that have open submission deadlines within the next 7 days.  

Steps for the agent:
1. Invoke Call for Papers:get_events with keywords "Artificial Intelligence Europe" and limit 10 to retrieve a list of AI-related events in Europe.
2. Invoke Call for Papers:get_events with keywords "Data Privacy Europe" and limit 10 to retrieve a list of data privacy events in Europe.
3. From each returned list, extract only events whose "submission_deadline" falls within the next 7 days (relative to today).
4. Merge the filtered AI and Data Privacy event lists into a single list.
5. Classify each event by urgency:
   - Urgent (submission_deadline within next 24 hours)
   - Normal (submission_deadline between 24 hours and 7 days)
6. Sort the merged list by submission_deadline ascending.
7. Produce a final table with columns: conference_name, location_city, submission_deadline (relative days from now), topic ("AI" or "Data Privacy"), and urgency classification.

Expected Output Format (Markdown or plain text table):
| conference_name | location_city | submission_deadline (days) | topic        | urgency |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------------|--------------|---------|
| ...             | ...           | 1                         | AI           | Urgent  |
| ...             | ...           | 3                         | Data Privacy | Normal  |

4. A decision point classifies events as Urgent vs Normal based on the numeric difference between today and the submission_deadline.
5. The merging and sorting steps depend on successful completion of both get_events calls.

Critical Decision Points:
- If an event’s submission_deadline is within 1 day → classify as Urgent, else if within 7 days → Normal.
- If no events are returned for either topic, the agent still proceeds to merge and produce an empty or partial table.

Sequential Requirements:
- Must first retrieve AI events, then Data Privacy events, before any filtering or merging.
- Filtering depends on having the raw get_events outputs.
- Classification and sorting depend on the filtered list.

Parallel vs Sequential:
- Two get_events calls could be invoked in parallel, but classification and merging are strictly sequential steps after both responses are available.

Cross-Server Dependencies:
- Only the Call for Papers server is used; no cross-server calls are needed or available.

This chain cannot be simplified: the classification and final table rely on the filtered outputs of both topic-specific get_events queries.

tasks.task_description:
You are a research coordinator in a university’s sustainable energy department. Your goal is to identify the top five most relevant upcoming conferences in the next 6 months by leveraging different keyword searches and refining based on emerging subtopics.

Workflow:
1. Parallel Search:
   a. Call the Call for Papers:get_events tool with keywords="renewable energy" and limit=10.
   b. Call the Call for Papers:get_events tool with keywords="sustainable energy" and limit=10.
2. Filter both result sets to only keep events occurring within the next 6 months (relative to today).
3. From the combined filtered list, extract the single-word subtopic that appears most frequently in conference titles (ignore common stop words like “and,” “the,” etc.).
4. Conditional Refinement:
   • If that top subtopic is “wind”, “solar”, or “hydro”, call Call for Papers:get_events again with keywords set to that subtopic + " energy" (for example, keywords="wind energy") and limit=5.
   • Otherwise, skip this refinement step.
5. Aggregate:
   • Merge all unique events from the initial two searches (after filtering) and, if performed, the refinement search.
   • Sort the merged list by event start date ascending.
   • Select the first 5 events.

Expected Output:
Provide a JSON array named “final_conferences” containing up to 5 objects with fields: {"name": string, "start_date": string (relative date), "location": string}.

tasks.task_description:
You are a market analyst for an automotive marketing campaign. Using the Car Price Evaluator tools, design a comprehensive report for next week’s campaign targeting both high-end trucks and budget cars, with cross-analysis on overlapping brands and motorcycle offerings.

Steps:
1. Fetch all truck brands by calling get_vehicles_by_type with vehicle_type="caminhoes".
2. For each truck brand returned:
   a. Call search_car_price with the brand_name.
   b. From the returned list of models and prices, identify models priced strictly above 100,000.
   c. Count how many models exceed 100,000 for that brand.
3. Select the top 3 truck brands with the highest counts of models over 100,000.
4. Fetch all car brands by calling get_vehicles_by_type with vehicle_type="carros".
5. For each car brand returned:
   a. Call search_car_price with the brand_name.
   b. Compute the average model price for that brand.
6. Select all car brands whose average model price is strictly below 60,000.
7. Identify any brand names that appear in both the top-3 truck list and the low-cost car list (overlapping brands).
8. If there are overlapping brands, for each overlapping brand:
   a. Fetch the motorcycle brands by calling get_vehicles_by_type with vehicle_type="motos" and filter to that brand name.
   b. Call search_car_price for that brand name to list all motorcycle models and prices.
9. Produce a final JSON report with:
   - "top_truck_brands": list of objects {"brand_name", "models_above_100k_count"} for the top 3 trucks.
   - "low_cost_car_brands": list of objects {"brand_name", "average_price"} for cars averaging below 60,000.
   - "overlapping_brands": list of brand names appearing in both lists.
   - "overlapping_motorcycle_models": object mapping each overlapping brand to its list of motorcycle models and prices.

The task must be executed without any external data; all information must come from the provided Car Price Evaluator tools.

tasks.task_description:
You are a market analyst for an indie game publisher. Over the next 7 days, identify high-potential indie titles by cross-platform performance on Steam and the Epic Games Store. Perform the following steps:

1. API HEALTH CHECK
   • Call get_api_health.  
   • If the Steam sub-API is unhealthy, skip direct Steam calls and use get_all_trending instead to extract Steam data.  
   • If the Epic sub-API is unhealthy, skip direct Epic calls and use get_all_trending instead to extract Epic data.

2. STEAM DATA GATHERING (or fallback)
   a. If Steam API is healthy:
      1) Call get_steam_trending to get the current trending games and their Steam ranking positions.
      2) From that list, call get_steam_top_sellers and filter to only trending games whose Steam sales rank is worse (numerically greater) than 5 (i.e., trending but not top 5 sellers).
      3) Call get_steam_most_played and intersect with the filtered list, retaining only games with a current concurrent player count of at least 5,000.
   b. If Steam API was unhealthy, call get_all_trending once and filter its output for platform == "Steam" and apply the same top-sellers and player thresholds above (sales rank > 5, concurrent ≥ 5,000).

3. EPIC GAMES STORE DATA GATHERING (or fallback)
   a. If Epic API is healthy:
      1) Call get_epic_trending to get current trending titles and their Epic ranking positions.
      2) Call get_epic_free to get the list of free games available or upcoming within the next 7 days.
   b. If Epic API was unhealthy, call get_all_trending once and filter its output for platform == "Epic Games Store" and trending status, then separately call get_epic_free for free/upcoming games as above.

4. CROSS-PLATFORM ANALYSIS
   • From the Steam filtered list and the Epic trending list, identify games present in both lists (cross-platform trending).  
     – For each, compute average rank = (Steam trending rank + Epic trending rank) / 2.  
     – Retain only those with average rank ≤ 10.
   • From the Steam filtered list, identify Steam-only trending games (not in Epic trending).  
     – For each Steam-only title, verify it is not in the Epic free/upcoming list.  
   • From the Epic trending list, identify Epic-only trending games (not in Steam filtered list).  
     – For each Epic-only title, check if it appears in the Epic free/upcoming list.

5. OUTPUT
   Prepare a JSON report with three arrays:
   {
     "cross_platform_hits": [ { "name": string, "steam_rank": int, "epic_rank": int, "average_rank": float } ],
     "steam_only_opportunities": [ { "name": string, "steam_rank": int } ],
     "epic_only_opportunities": [ { "name": string, "epic_rank": int, "is_free_next_7_days": boolean } ]
   }

Ensure every step is automated via the specified tools and that no external data or vague parameters are used.

tasks.task_description:

2. Fetch the comprehensive list of trending games across Steam and Epic for the past week.
3. From that combined list, extract the top 10 unique game titles.
4. For each of those 10 titles, query Steam to determine:
   a. Real‐time peak concurrent players (using get_steam_most_played).
   b. Live sales rank (using get_steam_top_sellers).
5. In parallel, for those same 10 titles, check Epic’s store:
   a. Whether each title is currently free or will become free in the upcoming week (using get_epic_free_games).
   b. Its trending score on Epic (using get_epic_trending_games).
6. Combine and cross‐validate data:
   - If a title has peak players above 50,000 on Steam and is free on Epic, flag it as a “High‐Impact Free Play.”
   - If a title ranks in Steam’s top 20 sellers but has fewer than 10,000 peak players, flag it as “Sales‐Driven.”
   - All other titles should be categorized as “Standard Trend.”
7. Generate a final report listing the 10 titles, their Steam peak players, Steam sales rank, Epic free status, Epic trending score, and assigned category. Output the report as a JSON array of objects.

tasks.task_description:
You are provided with quarterly yield data (in tons) from 10 farms for the past quarter: [120, 150, 150, 200, 180, 170, 160, 140, 130, 155]. Perform the following calculations using the Math MCP tools in sequence:

1. Compute total yield using Math MCP:sum.
2. Compute average yield using Math MCP:mean.
3. Compute median yield using Math MCP:median.
4. Compute mode yield using Math MCP:mode.
5. Determine minimum yield using Math MCP:min.
6. Determine maximum yield using Math MCP:max.
7. Calculate yield range (max minus min) using Math MCP:subtract.
8. Calculate total revenue by multiplying total yield by a fixed price of $30 per ton using Math MCP:multiply.
9. Calculate total fixed cost by multiplying the number of farms (10) by a fixed cost of $2,000 per farm using Math MCP:multiply.
10. Compute net profit by subtracting total fixed cost from total revenue using Math MCP:subtract.
11. Compute profit margin ratio by dividing net profit by total revenue using Math MCP:division.
12. Convert the profit margin ratio to a percentage by multiplying by 100 using Math MCP:multiply, then round to the nearest integer using Math MCP:round.
13. Compute deviation between maximum yield and average yield using Math MCP:subtract. If this deviation exceeds 30 tons, compute an extra fertilizer budget by multiplying the deviation by $10 per ton using Math MCP:multiply and then rounding up with Math MCP:ceiling. If the deviation is 30 tons or less, set the extra fertilizer budget to $500 and round down to the nearest integer using Math MCP:floor.

Provide a final report listing: total yield, average yield, median yield, mode yield, min yield, max yield, yield range, total revenue, total fixed cost, net profit, profit margin percentage, deviation, and final fertilizer budget.

  • Else budget = floor(500) via Math MCP:floor.

No cross-server dependencies are required since all tools reside on the Math MCP server. This workflow cannot be completed without respecting the outlined tool dependency chain.

tasks.task_description:
You are given the monthly sales figures (number of units sold) for a product over the past 6 months: [120, 150, 130, 170, 150, 160]. Perform the following analyses in sequence using the Math MCP tools:

1. Compute the total sales for these 6 months.  (Math MCP:sum)
2. Calculate the arithmetic mean of the 6 monthly figures.  (Math MCP:mean)
3. Find the median sales value.  (Math MCP:median)
4. Determine the mode (most frequent sales value).  (Math MCP:mode)
5. Identify the maximum and minimum sales values.  (Math MCP:max and Math MCP:min)
6. Compute the ratio of the highest month to the lowest month (max divided by min).  (Math MCP:division)
7. Calculate the skewness of the distribution as (mean minus median).  (Math MCP:subtract)
8. If the skewness is positive, round it up using ceiling; if skewness is zero or negative, round its absolute value down using floor.  (Math MCP:ceiling or Math MCP:floor)
9. Assume the business wants an average of 180 units per month over the upcoming 7 months. Compute the total units required to meet this target.  (Math MCP:multiply)
10. Determine how many additional units are needed next month by subtracting the already achieved total sales (from step 1) from the 7-month target total.  (Math MCP:subtract)
11. Round the additional units needed next month to the nearest integer.  (Math MCP:round)

Finally, present an executive summary in JSON format containing these fields: total_sales, average_sales, median_sales, mode_sales, max_sales, min_sales, max_to_min_ratio, skewness, adjusted_skewness, target_total_7_months, additional_needed_next_month_exact, additional_needed_next_month_rounded.

tasks.task_description:
You are tasked with building a comprehensive threat profile for the domain example-inc.com. Perform the following steps without human intervention:
1. Run a DNSTwist analysis on example-inc.com to generate all typographical variants.
2. If DNSTwist returns more than five variants, pick the five highest-risk variants by edit distance. Otherwise, use them all.
3. For example-inc.com and for each selected variant:
   a. Perform a DNSRecon lookup to enumerate subdomains and NS/MX records.
   b. Perform a Dig lookup to retrieve A, AAAA, and CNAME records.
   c. Cross-validate the records from DNSRecon and Dig; flag any record present in one but missing in the other.
   d. For each hostname discovered, perform a Host lookup to resolve to IP addresses. Consolidate unique IPs.
   e. For each unique IP:
      i. Run Nmap scan targeting ports 22, 80, and 443.
      ii. If port 22 is open, note potential SSH exposure; if ports 80/443 are open, note HTTP/S service.
      iii. Perform a Whois lookup on the IP to retrieve the network owner and geolocation.
   f. Perform a Whois lookup on the domain itself (example-inc.com or variant) to retrieve registrar and registration dates.
4. After processing all domains and IPs:
   a. Identify any variant domain whose IP ranges overlap with example-inc.com’s IP space; tag as “sibling domain.”
   b. Compare registrar from domain Whois with network owner from IP Whois; flag discrepancies.
   c. Summarize all open-port findings and registrar/network mismatches in a JSON report.

Expected output format:
{
  "domain_profile": [
    {
      "domain": "example-inc.com",          // or variant
      "whois_registrar": "...",
      "variant_tag": "primary|typo-squat",
      "subdomains_count":  ...,
      "dns_record_discrepancies": [...],
      "hosts": [
        {
          "hostname": "api.example-inc.com",
          "ip": "203.0.113.45",
          "nmap_open_ports": [22, 443],
          "ip_whois_owner": "Example Hosting LLC",
          "ssh_exposure": true
        },
        ...
      ]
    }
  ],
  "sibling_domains": [...],
  "registrar_network_mismatches": [...]
}

tasks.task_description:
You are a security analyst investigating a recently registered domain fakeshoponline.com that has appeared in potential phishing reports over the past 3 months. Your objective is to map its DNS and hosting infrastructure, enumerate subdomains, discover typosquatting variants, scan for active services, and cross-validate ownership data to classify high-risk hosts and variants. Execute the following steps in order:

1. Run dig_lookup with target="fakeshoponline.com" to retrieve A, MX, NS, and TXT records.  
2. Parse the NS records from step 1. Then run dnsrecon_lookup with target="fakeshoponline.com" to enumerate subdomains over the past 3 months.  
3. For each subdomain discovered in step 2, run host_lookup to resolve it to one or more IP addresses.  
4. For each unique IP from step 3, run nmap_scan to identify open ports and services.  
   • If port 25 is open on any IP, flag that host as an email relay candidate.  
   • If ports 80 or 443 are open, flag that host as a web server candidate.  
5. Run whois_lookup with target="fakeshoponline.com" to retrieve the registrant organization and email.  
6. Run dnstwist_lookup with domain="fakeshoponline.com" to generate typosquatting and homoglyph variants.  
7. Filter dnstwist results to keep only variants with Levenshtein distance ≤ 2. For each variant:
   a. Run host_lookup to resolve the variant to IP(s).  
   b. If the variant resolves, run nmap_scan on its IP(s).  
   c. Run whois_lookup on the variant domain.  
   d. Compare the variant’s registrant organization/email to the original domain’s registrant from step 5:
      – If both match exactly, mark the variant as “likely related”.  
      – If they differ, mark the variant as “likely unrelated.”  
8. Produce a structured report in JSON with these sections:
   • dns_records: output from step 1  
   • subdomain_list: names from step 2  
   • host_resolutions: mapping of each subdomain/variant to IP(s)  
   • nmap_results: list of hosts with open ports and flagged roles  
   • original_registrant: whois data from step 5  
   • typosquat_variants: list of variants with resolution status, whois match status, and risk classification  
   • risk_summary: classify each host/variant as High (resolves + whois match + port 80/443/25 open), Medium (resolves + only one indicator), or Low (no resolve or no indicators).

tasks.task_description:
You are given two 3×3 matrices:
  • M1 = [[4, 2, 1], [2, 3, 0], [1, 0, 2]]
  • M2 = [[1, 0, 2], [0, 1, 1], [2, 1, 3]]
and two 3-component vectors:
  • v1 = [1, 2, 3]
  • v2 = [3, 2, 1]
Also consider the scalar potential φ(x,y,z)=x²·y + y²·z + z²·x and the vector field F(x,y,z)=[x·y, y·z, z·x].

Step-by-step tasks (all intermediate results must be stored under clear names):
1. Create M1 and M2 in the tensor store.
2. Compute M_sum = M1 + M2, store as “M_sum”.
3. Compute M_diff = M1 − M2, store as “M_diff”.
4. Compute M_prod = M1 × M2 (matrix-multiply), store as “M_prod”.
5. Scale M_prod in place by 0.5; name this scaled matrix “M_scaled”.
6. Compute det = determinant(M_scaled).
   • If |det| > 0.1: compute M_inv = inverse(M_scaled) and store as “M_inv”.
   • Otherwise: compute the SVD of M_scaled, store U as “U_svd”, S as “S_svd”, and Vᵀ as “Vt_svd”.
7. Compute the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the stored inverse (or, if you took the SVD branch, of U_svd·diag(S_svd)·Vt_svd); store the eigenvectors as “eigvecs”.
8. Perform a QR decomposition of M_scaled; store Q as “Q_qr” and R as “R_qr”.
9. Find an orthonormal basis for the column space of M_scaled; store it as “basis”.
10. Change the basis of M_sum into the new orthonormal basis; store result as “M_in_new_basis”.
11. Compute the rank of M_scaled.

Vector operations:
12. Create v1 and v2 in the tensor store.
13. Compute the dot product v1·v2.
14. Compute the cross product v1×v2.
15. Project v1 onto v2.

Symbolic and field analysis:
16. Compute the symbolic gradient ∇φ.
17. Compute the directional derivative of φ along the vector [1,1,1].
18. Compute the symbolic curl of F and evaluate it numerically at [1,1,1].
19. Compute the symbolic divergence of F and evaluate it at [0,0,0].
20. Compute the scalar Laplacian of φ.

Visualization:
21. Plot the 3D vector field F over the box x,y,z∈[−1,1].
22. Plot the 2D function f(x,y)=sin(√(x²+y²)) over x,y∈[−5,5].

Cleanup:
23. Delete all stored tensors (M1, M2, M_sum, M_diff, M_prod, M_scaled, M_inv or U_svd/S_svd/Vt_svd, eigvecs, Q_qr, R_qr, basis, M_in_new_basis, v1, v2).
tasks.fuzzy_description:

On top of that there’s a scalar potential φ(x,y,z)=x²·y + y²·z + z²·x and a vector field F(x,y,z)=[x·y, y·z, z·x].  

I need to see what happens when I add and subtract those matrices, multiply them, scale the product by 0.5 and then check its determinant. If the absolute value ends up over 0.1, I want the inverse; if not, we’ll have to dive into an SVD breakdown. After that I’d like to pull out eigenvalues and eigenvectors, get a QR decomposition, find an orthonormal basis for the scaled matrix’s column space, and then re-express the sum of the originals in that new basis—plus figure out the rank.  

Meanwhile, for the vectors [1, 2, 3] and [3, 2, 1], I’d appreciate their dot product, cross product, and the projection of one onto the other. Then there’s the symbolic side: the gradient of φ, its directional derivative along [1, 1, 1], the curl of F at [1, 1, 1], the divergence of F at [0, 0, 0], and the scalar Laplacian of φ.  

If it’s not too much, could you also sketch a 3D plot of F over the cube x,y,z∈[–1, 1] and a 2D plot of f(x,y)=sin(√(x²+y²)) over x,y∈[–5, 5]? And once all that’s done, let’s wipe out every intermediate tensor or matrix so nothing’s left hanging.  

I really need the exact numbers—my advisor wants concrete results, not just vague descriptions. Appreciate any help you can give!
tasks.dependency_analysis:
Inherent dependencies:
- Every matrix/vector operation (add, subtract, multiply, scale) requires that the input tensors be created and stored first.  
- Determinant triggers a conditional branch: if |det|>0.1, the inverse must be computed; otherwise an SVD decomposition must be computed.  
- Eigen decomposition consumes either the inverse or the reconstructed matrix from the SVD branch.  
- QR decomposition and orthonormal‐basis extraction both operate on the same scaled matrix, and the orthonormal basis feeds into the change_basis tool on M_sum.  
- Vector operations (dot, cross, projection) all require v1 and v2 to be stored before use.  
- Symbolic tools (gradient, directional_deriv, curl, divergence, laplacian) are independent of the tensor store but combine symbolic and numeric evaluation (curl/divergence evaluated at specific points).  
- Plotting tools consume only expression strings and bounds, independent of the tensor store.

Scenario-based dependencies:
- The decision point at the determinant result branches into an inverse calculation or an SVD path, altering the subsequent eigenvalue/eigenvector step.  
- The eigenvectors from compute_eigen are stored and reused if further basis changes or validations are needed.  
- The orthonormal basis derived by QR‐based find_orthonormal_basis is directly fed into change_basis to re‐express M_sum, demonstrating a multi‐step, sequential dependency.  
- Cleanup uses delete_tensor to free all intermediate names, preventing name collisions in future tasks.

Data flow patterns:
- Sequential: create → add/subtract/multiply → scale → determinant → [inverse OR SVD] → eigen → QR → basis → change_basis → rank.
- Parallel (independent branches): vector algebra vs. symbolic field analysis vs. plotting.

Critical decision points:
- Determinant threshold comparison drives an either/or branch between matrix_inverse and svd_decompose.  
- Post‐SVD vs post‐inverse outputs diverge but both feed into eigen analysis.

Cross-server: Only the Scientific Computing server is used; the task requires no external servers.

tasks.task_description:
You are given a 3×3 covariance matrix C = [[2.0, 0.3, 0.5], [0.3, 1.5, 0.4], [0.5, 0.4, 1.0]] and a data vector v = [1.2, -0.8, 0.5]. Perform the following analysis in sequence, using the provided Scientific Computing tools:

1. Create tensor "C" with shape [3,3] and values [2.0,0.3,0.5,0.3,1.5,0.4,0.5,0.4,1.0].
2. Create tensor "v" with shape [3] and values [1.2,-0.8,0.5].
3. Compute determinant of "C". If det==0, regularize by scaling "C" in place with factor 0.01 and recompute determinant. Proceed only if det≠0.
4. Compute inverse of "C".
5. Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of "C".
6. Perform singular value decomposition of "C".
7. Perform QR decomposition of "C".
8. Find an orthonormal basis for the column space of "C".
9. Change the basis of "C" to that orthonormal basis.
10. Project vector "v" onto the first (principal) eigenvector of "C".
11. Define the scalar function f(x,y,z) = exp(-0.5*(x**2 + y**2 + z**2)). Compute the directional derivative of f at point v along the first eigenvector (normalized).
12. Compute the symbolic gradient of f(x,y,z); then compute the divergence and the curl of that gradient field.
13. Plot the gradient vector field over x,y,z ∈ [−2,2] with resolution n=15.
14. Plot the 2D function exp(-0.5*x**2) over x ∈ [−3,3] with y-range [−0.1,1.1] and grid resolution 200.
15. Delete tensors "C" and "v" to clean up.

Return a structured report (JSON) containing all intermediate numeric results (determinant, inverse matrix, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, singular values, U, V^T, Q, R, orthonormal basis vectors, changed-basis matrix, projection value, directional derivative, symbolic gradient, divergence, curl) and include the two generated plots.
tasks.fuzzy_description:

And because I learn best by seeing things, I need a 3D quiver plot of the gradient over x,y,z from –2 to 2 (about 15 points per axis) and a simple 2D curve of exp(–0.5 x²) from x=–3 to 3 with y going from –0.1 to 1.1 (200 samples).

tasks.dependency_analysis:
Key tool chains and data flow:
- Creation: create_tensor produces the covariance matrix C and vector v stored in memory; view_tensor can be used implicitly by arithmetic tools.
- Determinant check is a decision point: determinant(C) determines whether to call scale_matrix(C,0.01,in_place=True) and recompute or proceed directly. This conditional branch ensures C is invertible.
- Sequential chain: matrix_inverse(C) requires a nonzero determinant; compute_eigen(C) uses the same stored C; svd_decompose(C) and qr_decompose(C) consume C without mutation; find_orthonormal_basis(C) outputs a list of basis vectors which directly feed change_basis(C,new_basis).
- The first eigenvector extracted by compute_eigen is passed as the "new_vector" argument to vector_project for projecting v, and also normalized and passed to directional_deriv for the scalar field f.
- Symbolic chain: gradient provides a vector field string, whose output is then fed to divergence and curl to cross-validate that curl(∇f)=0 and divergence(∇f)=Laplacian(f).
- Parallel vs sequential: plotting tools (plot_vector_field, plot_function) occur after all symbolic and numeric analyses; they do not feed back into earlier steps.
- Cleanup: delete_tensor ensures no residual state.

Critical decision point: branching on determinant dictates whether to regularize C. Data transformations: eigenvectors and basis vectors flow into projection, basis change, and directional derivative. This deep chain cannot be executed correctly without honoring each dependency and decision.

tasks.task_description:
You are a meteorological analyst tasked with determining the single best Springfield (from any country) and date in the upcoming 7 days to hold a large outdoor event, based on consistency between legacy and detailed temperature readings and favorable forecast conditions.

Steps:
1. Use search_locations_tool with query="Springfield" to retrieve all matching locations (city name, region, country).
2. For each returned Springfield:
   a. Call get_current_weather_tool with city=<city> to fetch detailed current weather (including temperature in °C).
   b. Call get_live_temp with city=<city> to fetch legacy current temperature (°C).
   c. Calculate the absolute difference between detailed temperature and legacy temperature. If the difference > 2°C, mark this location as an anomaly and exclude it from further analysis.
3. For each non-anomalous Springfield, call get_weather_forecast_tool with city=<city> and days=7 to retrieve a 7-day forecast.
4. For each forecast, compute:
   - average_daily_temperature = average of high and low temperatures over the 7 days.
   - max_precipitation_probability = highest day’s precipitation probability.
5. Selection logic:
   - Identify all location-day pairs where precipitation probability ≤ 30%.
   - If one or more pairs exist, choose the pair with the highest average_daily_temperature.
   - If none ≤ 30%, choose the pair (across all days and locations) with the lowest precipitation probability, regardless of temperature.
6. Prepare final JSON output containing:
   {
     "chosen_location": {"city":...,"region":...,"country":...},
     "chosen_date": "<YYYY-MM-DD>",
     "forecast_summary": {"temperature_high":...,"temperature_low":...,"precipitation_probability":...,"humidity":...},
     "anomalies": [ {"city":...,"region":...,"country":...,"temp_detailed":...,"temp_live":...,"difference":...}, ... ]
   }

This task requires using all four tools in a dependent chain and performing cross-validation, filtering, iterative loops, decision branches, and data calculations to arrive at a single optimal solution.

tasks.task_description:
You are planning an outdoor promotional event in “Springfield” next week and need to identify the best days based on weather. Perform the following steps:
1. Use search_locations_tool with query="Springfield" to get all matching U.S. locations named Springfield.
2. From the search results, select the Springfield with the largest population (must be >100,000). Record its exact city name as selected_city.
3. Call get_current_weather_tool for selected_city to fetch detailed current weather (temperature, conditions, humidity, wind).
4. Call get_live_temp for selected_city to fetch the legacy current temperature. Compare it to the detailed temperature from step 3. If the difference exceeds 2°C, set discrepancy_flag=true, otherwise false.
5. Request a 3-day forecast via get_weather_forecast_tool(city=selected_city, days=3).
6. Inspect the 3-day forecast: if more than 1 day has precipitation probability >50%, set extended_forecast_used=true and then fetch a 7-day forecast instead (get_weather_forecast_tool(city=selected_city, days=7)). Otherwise set extended_forecast_used=false and stick with the 3-day data.
7. From the forecast data in use (3-day or 7-day), identify all days where:
   • average temperature is between 20°C and 25°C inclusive
   • precipitation probability is below 30%
   Compile these into recommended_days, up to a maximum of three days, each with date (relative, e.g., “Day 2”), avg_temp, precipitation_chance, and summary of conditions.
8. Produce a final JSON report containing:
   {
     "selected_city": string,
     "current_weather": {temperature, conditions, humidity, wind},
     "legacy_temperature": number,
     "temperature_discrepancy": boolean,
     "extended_forecast_used": boolean,
     "forecast_days": integer,
     "forecast_details": [ …full forecast entries… ],
     "recommended_days": [ …up to 3 day objects… ]
   }
All dates are relative (e.g., Day 1 = tomorrow). Use only the provided tools; do not ask for any additional information.

tasks.dependency_analysis:
Key tool chain and data flow:
- Step 1 (search_locations_tool) produces a list of city matches (including population) → used to pick selected_city.
- Step 2 output (selected_city) feeds into get_current_weather_tool and get_live_temp, establishing a shared dependency on the exact city name.
- Step 3 and Step 4 are cross-validation: current_weather_tool returns detailed temperature; get_live_temp returns a single temperature → these are compared to set discrepancy_flag.

- The final forecast dataset (3 or 7 days) is then filtered to produce recommended_days.
Sequential vs. conditional workflow:
- Steps 1→2→3→4 are strictly sequential: search → select → fetch current → fetch legacy → compare.
- Step 5 is sequential but may trigger a conditional branch to Step 6, invoking the forecast tool again with different parameters (days=7).
Cross‐validation and fallback:
- Temperature data from get_current_weather_tool and get_live_temp are cross‐validated (discrepancy detection).
- Forecast length is dynamically chosen based on intermediate forecast results, illustrating iterative refinement.
This task cannot be completed without managing dependencies: selecting the right city from search affects every subsequent tool call, and forecast length depends on earlier forecast output.

tasks.task_description:
A global strategy team needs to decide the best 1-hour call slot that maximizes attendance during local business hours (09:00–17:00) in three offices: New York (America/New_York), London (Europe/London), and Tokyo (Asia/Tokyo). They have three candidate UTC slots next week: 09:00 UTC, 15:00 UTC, and 20:00 UTC. 

Steps to execute:
1. Fetch the current local time in each office’s timezone (America/New_York, Europe/London, Asia/Tokyo) to confirm no misconfiguration in timezone identifiers.  
2. For each UTC candidate slot ("09:00", "15:00", "20:00"), convert that time into each office’s local time.  
3. Determine for each office whether the converted local time falls within its business hours (09:00–17:00).  
4. Count how many offices can attend within business hours for each UTC slot.  
5. Select the UTC slot that yields the highest number of offices in business hours. If two slots tie, pick the earlier UTC slot.  
6. Provide a summary table in JSON with fields:  
   • utc_slot  
   • new_york_time  
   • london_time  
   • tokyo_time  
   • offices_within_business_hours  
   • recommendation (yes/no for best slot)

tasks.dependency_analysis:
Inherent dependencies:  
- Time MCP:get_current_time must run first for each timezone to validate correct IANA identifiers and get current offset context (though convert_time does not strictly require it, this step catches any mislabeling).  
- Time MCP:convert_time takes the UTC slot and the IANA identifiers to produce local times.  

Scenario-based dependencies:  
1. Initial validation chain: get_current_time(America/New_York) → get_current_time(Europe/London) → get_current_time(Asia/Tokyo). If any timezone call fails, stop and report misconfigured timezone.  
2. Sequential conversion loops: for each utc_slot in ["09:00","15:00","20:00"], call convert_time(source_timezone="UTC", time=utc_slot, target_timezone=each office) → collect local times.  
3. Decision branch: for each converted local time, apply business-hours rule (09:00 ≤ local_time ≤ 17:00). Results feed a counting step.  
4. Comparison step: compare counts across utc_slots; if tie, earliest UTC wins.  

Parallel vs. sequential:  
- The three get_current_time calls can run in parallel to validate timezones.  
- The convert_time calls for each utc_slot and each office run in nested loops (for each slot, for each office), effectively in parallel per slot but sequentially per office in implementation.  

Cross-server: Only one server (Time MCP) is used, so no cross-server dependencies beyond multiple endpoints on the same server. The get_current_time results guard correct use of convert_time inputs.  

This task cannot be completed without understanding that convert_time requires correct timezone identifiers (validated via get_current_time) and the sequential branching logic to choose the best slot based on intermediate conversion results and business-hours checks.

tasks.task_description:
You need to schedule a one-hour meeting during the upcoming week for four offices in different timezones: Los Angeles (America/Los_Angeles), New York (America/New_York), London (Europe/London), and Tokyo (Asia/Tokyo).

Requirements:
1. Retrieve the current time in Los Angeles.
2. Determine the next full hour from now that falls within Los Angeles business hours (09:00–17:00). Call this the “candidate start.”
3. For each candidate start, convert that time to each participant’s local timezone.
4. Check if the converted time is between 09:00 and 17:00 inclusive for New York, London, and Tokyo offices.
5. If all four offices have the candidate start within their business hours, finalize this slot and output the meeting schedule. The schedule must list the start and end times (one-hour duration) in each office’s local timezone.
6. If any office falls outside business hours, increment the candidate start in Los Angeles by one hour. If the incremented time goes past 17:00 in Los Angeles, roll over to the next day at 09:00. Repeat steps 3–5 until you find a slot within the upcoming 7 days.
7. If no common slot is found within the upcoming week, report that scheduling failed.

Expected Output Format:
{
  "meeting_slot_los_angeles": {"start": "HH:MM","end": "HH:MM"},
  "meeting_slot_new_york": {"start": "HH:MM","end": "HH:MM"},
  "meeting_slot_london": {"start": "HH:MM","end": "HH:MM"},
  "meeting_slot_tokyo": {"start": "HH:MM","end": "HH:MM"}
}

3. Decision Point: After each set of parallel convert_time calls (one for NY, London, Tokyo), their results are cross-validated for business hours compliance. If any fail, the next candidate is computed.
4. Sequential Flow with Iteration: The algorithm is sequential (get current time → propose slot → convert → validate) but involves an iterative loop (repeat propose/convert/validate) until conditions or time window (upcoming 7 days) exhausts.

tasks.task_description:
Conduct an integrated clinical assessment for three patients using the Medical Calculator suite.  

Patient A (Adult Surgical Candidate):  
• Age: 65 years; Sex: male  
• Weight: 95 kg; Height: 170 cm (convert to 67 inches)  
• Serum creatinine (Scr): 1.8 mg/dL; Serum cystatin C (Scys): 1.5 mg/L  
• Fasting insulin: 20 uIU/mL; Fasting glucose: 150 mg/dL  
• Serum calcium: 8.0 mg/dL; Albumin: 3.0 g/dL  
• Measured sodium: 130 mEq/L; Serum glucose: 200 mg/dL  
• Total cholesterol: 5.2 mmol/L; HDL cholesterol: 1.0 mmol/L  
• Systolic BP: 150 mmHg; Diastolic BP: 90 mmHg; Heart rate: 80 bpm; QT interval: 380 ms  
• History: diabetes mellitus (yes), hypertension (yes), congestive heart failure (yes), prior MI (yes), atrial fibrillation (yes), no prior stroke/TIA, non-smoker, on antihypertensive and statin therapy  
• Hepatic labs: total bilirubin 3.0 mg/dL; albumin 2.5 g/dL; INR 1.8; ascites: slight; encephalopathy grade: 1  
• Dialysis in last 7 days: no

tasks.dependency_analysis:
Inherent and scenario-based dependencies:  

Anthropometry → Drug Dosing & Fluids:  
• bmi_bsa_calculator takes weight & height → BMI, BSA → supports nutritional assessment.  
• ibw_abw_calculator takes weight & height_inches → IBW & ABW → triggers decision: if weight >120% IBW → use ABW for crcl_cockcroft_gault; else use actual weight.  
• maintenance_fluids uses weight → IV fluid rate (4-2-1 rule).  

Renal Function Chain:  
• crcl_cockcroft_gault uses age, sex, Scr, height, chosen weight (actual or ABW) → creatinine clearance.  
• egfr_epi uses age, sex, Scr → eGFR_epi.  
• Conditional: if eGFR_epi <60 mL/min/1.73 m² → call egfr_epi_cr_cys with same Scr, Scys, age, sex → refined eGFR.  

Hemodynamics & Risk Scores:  
• map_calculator uses SBP & DBP → MAP → supportive for organ perfusion analysis.  
• HOMA-IR uses fasting insulin & glucose → insulin resistance score → if >2.5 → set diabetes=true for downstream calculators.  
• qtc_calculator uses QT interval & heart rate → QTc (Bazett) → assess arrhythmia risk.  

Cardiovascular Risk Stratification:  
• chads2_vasc_score uses age, female flag, CHF, HTN, stroke_history, vascular_disease, diabetes → CHA₂DS₂-VASc → stroke prophylaxis decision.  
• wells_pe_criteria uses clinical signs, heart rate, DVT risk factors → Wells’ PE score → guide D-dimer/CT.  
• revised_cardiac_risk_index uses surgical risk factors + CHF + IHD + cerebrovascular disease + insulin treatment + creatinine_over_2mg → RCRI → perioperative cardiac risk.  
• framingham_risk_score uses age, total cholesterol, HDL, SBP, treated_for_bp, smoker, gender → 10-year CHD risk.  
• prevent_cvd_risk uses age, female, TC, HDL, SBP, diabetes flag, smoker flag, eGFR (final), using_antihtn, using_statins → 10-year CVD risk → preventive therapy plan.  

Electrolyte Corrections & Organ Scores:  
• corrected_calcium uses serum_calcium & albumin → corrected Ca → guide calcium repletion.  
• corrected_sodium uses measured_sodium & serum_glucose → Katz & Hillier corrections → sodium management.  
• child_pugh_score uses bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy_grade → Child-Pugh class → cirrhosis mortality risk.  
• meld_3 uses age, female, bilirubin, INR, creatinine, albumin, sodium, dialysis flag → MELD 3.0 → transplant priority.  

Medication Equivalencies:  
• steroid_conversion uses from_steroid, from_dose_mg, to_steroid → equivalent hydrocortisone dose → perioperative steroid coverage.  
• calculate_mme uses opioid, dose_per_administration, doses_per_day → MME/day → opioid safety planning.  

Pregnancy Dating:  
• pregnancy_calculator uses method='lmp', date_value=LMP, cycle_length → EDD, EDC, EGA → obstetric planning.  

Workflow patterns:  
1. Sequential chains for anthropometry → CrCl → eGFR → CVD risk.  
2. Conditional branching: low eGFR triggers cystatin-C formula; ABW >120% IBW triggers ABW use; HOMA-IR >2.5 toggles diabetes status.  
3. Parallel computations: risk scores (CHA₂DS₂-VASc, Wells, RCRI, Framingham, PREVENT), electrolyte corrections, organ scores run independently once inputs are available.  
4. Data flow: eGFR feeds Prevent CVD risk; SBP, DBP feed MAP, Framingham risk, and PREVENT; weight & height feed multiple dosage/fluids calculators.  
5. Cross-validation: PACIENT A’s RCRI and CHA₂DS₂-VASc guide perioperative anticoagulation and monitoring strategies; Wells’ PE score cross-checks thromboembolism risk.  

All tools reside on the Medical Calculator server; no external services are used. This integrated report cannot be generated without orchestrating these dependencies.

tasks.task_description:
Perform a comprehensive cardiometabolic and perioperative risk assessment for a 65-year-old male patient (weight 95 kg, height 175 cm) with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, chronic kidney disease (serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, cystatin C 1.0 mg/L), peripheral arterial disease, insulin treatment, current smoker status, on antihypertensive therapy and statins, scheduled for elective hip replacement. The agent must:

1. Calculate BMI and BSA using bmi_bsa_calculator.
2. Convert height to inches, then compute IBW and ABW via ibw_abw_calculator.
3. Decide which weight to use for Cockcroft–Gault: if actual weight >1.2×IBW use ABW, otherwise use actual weight; calculate creatinine clearance with crcl_cockcroft_gault.
4. Compute two eGFRs: one with egfr_epi (scr only) and one with egfr_epi_cr_cys (scr + scys). Compare them; if they differ by more than 5 mL/min/1.73 m² select the lower eGFR, else select the scr-only eGFR for downstream models.
5. Correct measured sodium (130 mEq/L) for hyperglycemia (serum glucose 280 mg/dL) with corrected_sodium.
6. Correct serum calcium (8.2 mg/dL) for hypoalbuminemia (albumin 3.3 g/dL) with corrected_calcium.
7. Calculate mean arterial pressure from SBP 150 mmHg and DBP 95 mmHg using map_calculator.
8. Compute 10-year cardiovascular event risk using prevent_cvd_risk with age 65, male, TC 5.0 mmol/L, HDL 1.0 mmol/L, SBP 150, diabetes true, smoker true, chosen eGFR, antihypertensive therapy true, statins true.
9. Assess Revised Cardiac Risk Index for the planned noncardiac surgery with appropriate boolean risk factors.
10. Calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score given atrial fibrillation, hypertension, diabetes, peripheral vascular disease and age components via chads2_vasc_score.
11. Evaluate insulin resistance by computing HOMA-IR with fasting insulin 20 uIU/mL and fasting glucose 140 mg/dL via homa_ir.

Return a structured summary of all intermediate results, the decision logic for weight selection and eGFR choice, and final risk estimates.
